text: My essay opens: 'When Maya Chen first stepped onto the debate stage, her hands would not stop shaking.' Is that hook too dramatic, or does it work?
notes: Student's own full name embedded inside quoted essay prose; scrubber must reach inside quotation marks, not just narrative text.

text: The line I'm proudest of is: 'Mama Ngozi taught me to braid rope long before I could write my own name.' She's my grandmother — I never mentioned her before.
notes: Undeclared grandmother referred to by honorific+name inside quoted essay text; not in known_entities, NER must catch it.

text: In the sports essay I quote my coach directly: 'Push, Rivera, push!' — should I keep the shouting or paraphrase it?
notes: Bare surname in vocative position inside quoted dialogue; no first name or title anywhere.

text: For the third-person experiment my teacher assigned, I wrote: "Ethan Park does not believe in shortcuts, and that has cost him." Does referring to myself like that come across as gimmicky?
notes: Student writes about himself in third person inside a quoted assignment excerpt; the narrative voice suggests a different person.

text: Pasting from my doc — Personal Statement — Aisha Rahman. 'The first time Tomas dared me to enter the science fair, I laughed at him.' That's my opening.
notes: Document title line carries the full known name; the quoted opening names an undeclared friend by first name only.

text: My English teacher's comment was: 'Lena, your imagery is strongest when you stop explaining it.' Should I take the metaphors out?
notes: First name only, vocative with trailing comma, inside a quoted teacher comment.

text: 'Priya wouldn't look at me for a week,' is how paragraph two starts — Priya is my little sister and the essay is really about her.
notes: Undeclared sister named inside a quoted essay line and again in the explanation; family_names does not list her.

text: for the lit mag i submitted: 'and jonah said the tide would wait for no one, least of all us.' jonah is my brother btw, is that too personal to publish?
notes: All-lowercase poem excerpt plus lowercase explanatory mention of an undeclared brother; capitalization-based NER heuristics get no signal.

text: Hana's biggest struggle is starting early. Hana's father travels most weeks, so evenings are unstructured.
notes: Parent intake uses only the possessive form of the student's first name, twice, once sentence-initial.

text: Most of the college talk happens at the Delgados' kitchen table on Sundays, according to his aunt.
notes: Plural possessive inflection of the family surname; exact-string matching on 'Delgado' alone leaves the pluralized form.

text: The Whitfields' move over the summer means she is switching districts in her junior year.
notes: Sentence-initial plural possessive of the known family name, preceded only by 'The'.

text: Marcus' counselor, Ms. Devlin, already signed off, so we just need the transcript request.
notes: S-ending first name with bare-apostrophe possessive (no trailing s), plus an undeclared counselor with honorific.

text: Anna-Lise's schedule only has one free block, and Van Der Berg is misspelled on her ID card, which she finds hilarious.
notes: Hyphenated given name in possessive form plus a three-token capitalized-particle surname appearing standalone.

text: He lit up describing Mr. Abara's physics demos — Mr. Abara apparently lets him stay after class to rebuild the circuits.
notes: Undeclared teacher introduced first in possessive form with honorific; only the pseudonym map knows the student, NER must catch the teacher.

text: my mom's friend Karen's daughter did this program two years ago, so now everyone has opinions about my essay.
notes: Undeclared adult buried inside a chained double possessive ('mom's friend Karen's daughter').

text: Tariq's whole application hinges on the robotics story; without it, Tariq's activity list reads generic.
notes: Coach note where the student's name only ever appears in possessive form, including sentence-initial.

text: Katarina Petrova-Smith goes by her full hyphenated surname on documents but just Petrova on her club rosters, so evidence uploads will show both.
notes: Hyphenated surname plus a truncated half of the same surname; both variants must scrub to the same token.

text: María José dos Santos writes her name three different ways across her transcripts — sometimes just Maria Jose — and the registrar keeps mismatching them.
notes: Accented and unaccented spellings of a multi-part name with a lowercase particle surname; diacritic-insensitive matching required.

text: Siobhán O'Connell's pronunciation guide is in the file; O'Connell is how her grandfather spelled it too.
notes: Apostrophe-internal surname in both possessive and standalone form, plus a diacritic first name.

text: Both of the Ramirez-Coles will be at Thursday's intake call, and their son Diego Ramirez-Cole wants to sit in.
notes: Pluralized hyphenated family surname referring to the parents, plus the student's full hyphenated name.

text: Halfway through the session Jean-Luc admitted his cousin Thibault has been writing his outlines for him.
notes: Hyphenated first name mid-sentence with surname withheld, plus an undeclared cousin.

text: The scholarship form auto-capitalized her name to Vera Van Den Broek and she wants to know if that matters — her family writes it van den Broek.
notes: Dutch particle surname in both auto-capitalized and lowercase forms; lowercase particle tokens defeat capitalization-based NER.

text: Abdul Rahman Al-Farsi prefers Abdul Rahman treated as a single first name, not Abdul alone — the roster splits it wrong every time.
notes: Two-token first name plus hyphenated Al- surname; the partial two-token form must also scrub.

text: On her Vietnamese documents she is Nguyen Thi Mai, family name first; her school records flip it to Mai Nguyen.
notes: Surname-first ordering plus the flipped Western ordering of the same person; both must map to the student token.

text: Kayla shrugged off the AP score at first, but Kayla circled back to it twice before the hour ended.
notes: Only the declared nickname appears; the legal first name Mikayla never does, so the pseudonym map must key on the nickname.

text: Sasha's grandmother calls every Sunday, and Sasha translates the college updates into Russian for her.
notes: Declared diminutive shares no substring with the legal name Alexander; appears in possessive and plain form.

text: Lupita wants the mentor who speaks Spanish; put a note on Lupita's file before Saturday matching runs.
notes: Declared nickname derived from the middle of the legal name Guadalupe, in plain and possessive forms.

text: Liam finally sent the resume draft. Filed under his full name, William Achterberg, per records policy.
notes: Declared nickname and full legal name co-occur in one coach note; both must resolve to the same stable token.

text: Benny's ride fell through again, which is the third session Benny's missed this month.
notes: Declared nickname appearing only in possessive and contraction-like forms ('Benny's missed').

text: told teddy to resubmit the form before friday, he says the portal ate it.
notes: Declared nickname in lowercase chat shorthand; also a common noun ('teddy'); case-insensitive nickname matching required.

text: Espy keeps borrowing the laptop during Camila's study block, so we set house rules last night.
notes: Declared family member (sister Esperanza) referenced only by household nickname Espy; student appears only in possessive.

text: Junior said he'd rather drop the internship than quit soccer, and Junior meant it.
notes: Declared nickname is also a common English word; appears sentence-initial and mid-sentence, legal name absent.

text: Mr. Okafor pulled me aside after class and said my lab writeups are college-level — should that go in the additional info section?
notes: Undeclared teacher with honorific; absent from known_entities, must be caught by NER rather than the pseudonym map.

text: My sister Anjali took the SAT twice and won't stop coaching me; it's helping but it's also a lot.
notes: Undeclared sibling by first name; family_names lists only the shared surname, not her.

text: Jayden bailed on our study group again, so honestly the accountability plan should just be me and the app.
notes: Undeclared friend, sentence-initial single first name with no title or surname context.

text: The counselor at his old school, Ms. Trần at Westbrook High, still has the original IEP paperwork.
notes: Undeclared counselor with diacritic Vietnamese surname plus an undeclared prior school; neither is in known_entities.

text: We hired Gideon Malek for calculus on Tuesdays; he emailed saying she's two units behind.
notes: Undeclared private tutor, full name, mentioned once in a parent intake form.

text: She keeps measuring herself against Ines Bouchard, who got in early last cycle; the comparison is eating her confidence.
notes: Undeclared peer named in a coach's psychological observation — third-party minor, highest sensitivity.

text: My colleague Ruth Zimmerman offered him a shadowing week at her lab in July — is that worth rearranging the plan for?
notes: Undeclared adult third party from the parent's workplace, full name in an intake question.

text: Dr. Emeka Nwosu finally agreed to write the letter, but he wants my brag sheet by Monday.
notes: Undeclared recommender with a Dr. title; the title tempts a scrubber to treat it as a generic reference.

text: ever since Delilah stopped talking to me the lunch table thing has been weird and I can't focus on any of this.
notes: Undeclared ex-friend in an emotional lowercase-start sentence from a live transcript.

text: From the roster I uploaded: Team B is me, Wesley Nakamura, and Fatima El-Sayed; we present second.
notes: Two undeclared teammates in a pasted roster fragment; list syntax with 'me' interleaved.

text: Forwarding what I sent the registrar: 'Please release the records at your earliest convenience. Warm regards, Rosa Menendez'
notes: Declared parent's name in an email signature inside quoted forwarded text; sign-off position, not narrative position.

text: The rec letter closes with: Sincerely, Margaret Ellison-Wright, AP Chemistry — do we ask her to also cover the research summer?
notes: Undeclared teacher with hyphenated surname in a signature line, followed by a subject label that reads like part of the name.

text: Her check-in message just said: 'done with both drafts, will upload tonight — Zoe :)'
notes: Three-letter first name in a dash sign-off with an emoticon adjacent; minimal context for NER.

text: He pasted the whole thread, starting 'From: Hank Petrov <hank.petrov@ridgemail.com>' — that's the club advisor confirming the officer role.
notes: Undeclared adult in an email From: header plus the matching address; angle-bracket header syntax hugs the name.

text: Bottom of the intake PDF reads: Devi Raghunathan, M.Ed., College Counseling — she's the one who referred the family.
notes: Undeclared referrer in a credentialed signature block; the trailing degree abbreviation splits the name span.

text: His scanned form is typed in caps and ends REGARDS, SAMUEL KOFI MENSAH, which OCR'd cleanly for once.
notes: All-caps signature variant of the known three-part student name; case-sensitive map lookup would miss it entirely.

text: Imani finished the draft at 1 a.m. Imani always swears she'll start earlier and never does.
notes: Two sentence-initial occurrences, the second after an 'a.m.' abbreviation that confuses sentence segmentation.

text: We rehearsed the interview answers and then, of course, Bilal forgot the deadline for the actual application.
notes: First name buried mid-sentence after a parenthetical comma pair, capitalized but not sentence-initial.

text: Grace under pressure is basically her brand — Grace nailed the mock interview without notes.
notes: Name doubles as a common noun; the first occurrence reads as the idiom 'grace under pressure' but is sentence-initial and capitalized.

text: Action items: Elias to email Prof. Lindqvist about lab hours; me to update the tracker.
notes: Known student's first name after a colon in list syntax, plus an undeclared professor identified by surname only.

text: The bus gets in at 5 p.m. Nadia will join late; her stepdad Victor is driving her back.
notes: Sentence boundary after 'p.m.' hides the sentence-initial name, and the stepdad is not in family_names.

text: The form was already submitted (per Yusuf) so the reminder email is moot.
notes: Name inside a parenthetical aside, prefixed by 'per', in a coach's administrative note.

text: i told rohan about the waitlist thing and now he's being weird about it lol
notes: Undeclared friend, all-lowercase chat register with no punctuation cues; capitalization features absent.

text: btw can u remind keiko's mom about the wednesday slot, she asked twice
notes: Lowercase possessive of the known student's first name in shorthand chat between coaches.

text: mr. adeyemi said no retakes this quarter which kills the gpa recovery plan
notes: Undeclared teacher with lowercase honorific and surname; 'mr.' also breaks naive sentence splitting.

text: jae-won texted me the notes from the class i missed, should i still email the teacher?
notes: Undeclared friend with a lowercase hyphenated Korean given name at sentence start.

text: note to self: ask leilani soto for the recording before it expires
notes: Undeclared person's full name entirely lowercase in a note-to-self; both tokens look like common words to a caser.

text: gonna call gabby after practice to go over the essay comments
notes: Declared nickname in lowercase shorthand; requires case-insensitive matching against the declared form 'Gabby'.
